quotes.text: I was doing session work for almost four years before anyone in the room knew my name. I think that actually helped. I wasn't trying to impress the song.
quotes.speaker: H.E.R.
quotes.context: Interview with Zane Lowe on Apple Music 1, broadcast in conjunction with the Back of My Mind album release, June 2021
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — H.E.R. has spoken extensively about her years doing anonymous session and demo work before her identity became public, so this fits her documented biography exactly. The giveaway is the last line — 'I wasn't trying to impress the song' edges toward the kind of polished insight that feels slightly more rehearsed than real off-the-cuff speech.

quotes.text: I don't really listen back to records I've made. Maybe once, just to make sure the thing got out of the studio in one piece, and then that's it — it belongs to whoever's putting it on.
quotes.speaker: Jack White
quotes.context: Interview with Rolling Stone, published in the magazine's October 2022 print edition ahead of the Entering Heaven Alive release
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — Jack genuinely talks about detachment from finished work and his almost anti-nostalgic relationship with his own catalog, so this slots right in. The subtle giveaway is the phrase 'got out of the studio in one piece,' which is a little too tidy even for him.

quotes.text: My grandmother had a record player that sat on top of the refrigerator, and I remember standing on a step stool just to watch the needle. I thought something was alive in there. I still sort of think that.
quotes.speaker: Alicia Keys
quotes.context: Conversation with Oprah Winfrey on the Oprah's SuperSoul podcast, episode released March 2021
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — Alicia talks often about her early musical memories and her grandmother's influence, which makes this feel completely at home. The giveaway is that the image is a touch too cinematic — the step stool detail is just a hair too composed for real off-the-cuff memory.

quotes.text: I've been told my whole career that the bass is supposed to support. Support what? Support who? I want the bass to be the thing people are trying to survive.
quotes.speaker: Thundercat
quotes.context: Feature interview with Pitchfork, published online ahead of the It Is What It Is album release, April 2020
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — Thundercat talks constantly about reframing the bass guitar as a lead instrument and pushing back on conventional low-end thinking, so this fits his documented philosophy perfectly. The tiny giveaway is 'trying to survive,' which is just slightly more confrontational than his usual warm, slightly absurdist phrasing.

quotes.text: I've probably listened to it 10,000 times, and I still love it
quotes.speaker: Dave Grohl
quotes.context: Dave Grohl describing what he considers the perfect record

source.title: Music — Dave Grohl reveals what he believes to be the “perfect record”

quotes.text: He had the right attitude. He chewed gum, looked arrogant and flipped the bird to the guards – he did everything the prisoners wanted to do. He was a mean mother from the South who was there because he loved us. When he walked away, everyone in that place had become a Johnny Cash fan.
quotes.speaker: Merle Haggard
quotes.context: Recalling watching Johnny Cash perform at San Quentin prison on New Year's Day while Haggard was an inmate there.

source.title: Louder — Johnny Cash, Merle Haggard, and the true story that's almost too good to be true
